lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Some officers take them away: good guard,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Until their greater pleasures first be known

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: That are to censure them.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: We are not the first

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Who, with best meaning, have incurr’d the worst.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: For thee, oppressed king, am I cast down;

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Myself could else out-frown false fortune’s frown.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Shall we not see these daughters and these sisters?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, no, no, no! Come, let’s away to prison:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: We two alone will sing like birds i’ the cage:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: When thou dost ask me blessing, I’ll kneel down,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And ask of thee forgiveness: so we’ll live,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And pray, and sing, and tell old tales, and laugh

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: At gilded butterflies, and hear poor rogues

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Talk of court news; and we’ll talk with them too,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Who loses and who wins; who’s in, who’s out;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And take upon’s the mystery of things,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: As if we were God’s spies: and we’ll wear out,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: In a wall’d prison, packs and sects of great ones,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That ebb and flow by the moon.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Take them away.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Upon such sacrifices, my Cordelia,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The gods themselves throw incense. Have I caught thee?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: He that parts us shall bring a brand from heaven,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And fire us hence like foxes. Wipe thine eyes;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The good-years shall devour them, flesh and fell,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Ere they shall make us weep: we’ll see ’em starve first.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Come.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Come hither, captain; hark.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Take thou this note; go follow them to prison:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: One step I have advanced thee; if thou dost

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: As this instructs thee, thou dost make thy way

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: To noble fortunes: know thou this, that men

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Are as the time is: to be tender-minded

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Does not become a sword: thy great employment

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Will not bear question; either say thou’lt do ’t,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Or thrive by other means.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: I’ll do ’t, my lord.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: About it; and write happy when thou hast done.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Mark, I say, instantly; and carry it so

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: As I have set it down.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: I cannot draw a cart, nor eat dried oats;

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: If it be man’s work, I’ll do ’t.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Sir, you have shown to-day your valiant strain,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: And fortune led you well: you have the captives

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: That were the opposites of this day’s strife:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: We do require them of you, so to use them

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: As we shall find their merits and our safety

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: May equally determine.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Sir, I thought it fit

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: To send the old and miserable king

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: To some retention and appointed guard;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Whose age has charms in it, whose title more,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: To pluck the common bosom on his side,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: An turn our impress’d lances in our eyes

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Which do command them. With him I sent the queen;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: My reason all the same; and they are ready

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: To-morrow, or at further space, to appear

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Where you shall hold your session. At this time

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: We sweat and bleed: the friend hath lost his friend;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: And the best quarrels, in the heat, are cursed

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: By those that feel their sharpness:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: The question of Cordelia and her father

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Requires a fitter place.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Sir, by your patience,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: I hold you but a subject of this war,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Not as a brother.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: That’s as we list to grace him.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Methinks our pleasure might have been demanded,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Ere you had spoke so far. He led our powers;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Bore the commission of my place and person;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: The which immediacy may well stand up,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: And call itself your brother.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Not so hot:

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: In his own grace he doth exalt himself,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: More than in your addition.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: In my rights,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: By me invested, he compeers the best.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: That were the most, if he should husband you.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Jesters do oft prove prophets.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Holla, holla!

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: That eye that told you so look’d but a-squint.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Lady, I am not well; else I should answer

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: From a full-flowing stomach. General,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Take thou my soldiers, prisoners, patrimony;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Dispose of them, of me; the walls are thine:

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Witness the world, that I create thee here

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: My lord and master.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Mean you to enjoy him?

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: The let-alone lies not in your good will.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Nor in thine, lord.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Half-blooded fellow, yes.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: To Edmund. Let the drum strike, and prove my title thine.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Stay yet; hear reason. Edmund, I arrest thee

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: On capital treason; and, in thine attaint,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: This gilded serpent

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: For your claim, fair sister,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: I bar it in the interest of my wife:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: ’Tis she is sub-contracted to this lord,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: And I, her husband, contradict your bans.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: If you will marry, make your loves to me,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: My lady is bespoke.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: An interlude!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Thou art arm’d, Gloucester: let the trumpet sound:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: If none appear to prove upon thy head

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Thy heinous, manifest, and many treasons,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: There is my pledge; Throwing down a glove.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: I’ll prove it on thy heart,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Ere I taste bread, thou art in nothing less

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Than I have here proclaim’d thee.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Sick, O, sick!

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: If not, I’ll ne’er trust medicine.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: There’s my exchange: Throwing down a glove.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: what in the world he is

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: That names me traitor, villain-like he lies:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Call by thy trumpet: he that dares approach,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: On him, on you, who not? I will maintain

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: My truth and honour firmly.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: A herald, ho!

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: A herald, ho, a herald!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Trust to thy single virtue; for thy soldiers,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: All levied in my name, have in my name

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Took their discharge.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: My sickness grows upon me.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: She is not well; convey her to my tent.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: Sound, trumpet! A trumpet sounds.

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: “If any man of quality or degree within the lists of the

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: army will maintain upon Edmund, supposed Earl of Gloucester, that he is

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: a manifold traitor, let him appear by the third sound of the

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: trumpet: he is bold in his defence.”

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Sound! First trumpet.

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: Again! Second trumpet.

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: Again! Third trumpet.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Ask him his purposes, why he appears

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Upon this call o’ the trumpet.

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: What are you?

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: Your name, your quality? and why you answer

lines.speaker: HERALD
lines.text: This present summons?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Know, my name is lost;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: By treason’s tooth bare-gnawn and canker-bit:

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Yet am I noble as the adversary

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: I come to cope.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Which is that adversary?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: What’s he that speaks for Edmund Earl of Gloucester?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Himself: what say’st thou to him?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Draw thy sword,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: That, if my speech offend a noble heart,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Thy arm may do thee justice: here is mine.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Behold, it is the privilege of mine honours,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: My oath, and my profession: I protest,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Maugre thy strength, youth, place, and eminence,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Despite thy victor sword and fire-new fortune,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Thy valour and thy heart, thou art a traitor;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: False to thy gods, thy brother, and thy father;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Conspirant ’gainst this high-illustrious prince;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: And, from the extremest upward of thy head

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: To the descent and dust below thy foot,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: A most toad-spotted traitor. Say thou “No,”

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: This sword, this arm, and my best spirits, are bent

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: To prove upon thy heart, whereto I speak,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Thou liest.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: In wisdom I should ask thy name;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: But, since thy outside looks so fair and warlike,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: And that thy tongue some say of breeding breathes,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: What safe and nicely I might well delay

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: By rule of knighthood, I disdain and spurn:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Back do I toss these treasons to thy head;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: With the hell-hated lie o’erwhelm thy heart;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Which, for they yet glance by and scarcely bruise,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: This sword of mine shall give them instant way,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Where they shall rest for ever. Trumpets, speak!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Save him, save him!

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: This is practise, Gloucester:

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: By the law of arms thou wast not bound to answer

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: An unknown opposite; thou art not vanquish’d,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: But cozen’d and beguiled.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Shut your mouth, dame,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Or with this paper shall I stop it: Hold, sir:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Thou worse than any name, read thine own evil:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: No tearing, lady: I perceive you know it. Gives the letter to Edmund.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Say, if I do, the laws are mine, not thine:

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Who can arraign me for’t.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Most monstrous! oh!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Know’st thou this paper?

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Ask me not what I know.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Go after her: she’s desperate; govern her.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: What you have charged me with, that have I done;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: And more, much more; the time will bring it out:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: ’Tis past, and so am I. But what art thou

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: That hast this fortune on me? If thou’rt noble,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I do forgive thee.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Let’s exchange charity.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: I am no less in blood than thou art, Edmund;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: If more, the more thou hast wrong’d me.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: My name is Edgar, and thy father’s son.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The gods are just, and of our pleasant vices

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Make instruments to plague us:

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The dark and vicious place where thee he got

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Cost him his eyes.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Thou hast spoken right, ’tis true;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: The wheel is come full circle: I am here.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Methought thy very gait did prophesy

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: A royal nobleness: I must embrace thee:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Let sorrow split my heart, if ever I

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Did hate thee or thy father!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Worthy prince, I know’t.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Where have you hid yourself?

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: How have you known the miseries of your father?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: By nursing them, my lord. List a brief tale;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: And when ’tis told, O, that my heart would burst!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The bloody proclamation to escape,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: That follow’d me so near—O, our lives’ sweetness!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: That we the pain of death would hourly die

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Rather than die at once!—taught me to shift

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Into a madman’s rags; to assume a semblance

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: That very dogs disdain’d: and in this habit

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Met I my father with his bleeding rings,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Their precious stones new lost: became his guide,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Led him, begg’d for him, saved him from despair;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Never—O fault!—reveal’d myself unto him,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Until some half-hour past, when I was arm’d:

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Not sure, though hoping, of this good success,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: I ask’d his blessing, and from first to last

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Told him my pilgrimage: but his flaw’d heart,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Alack, too weak the conflict to support!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: ’Twixt two extremes of passion, joy and grief,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Burst smilingly.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: This speech of yours hath moved me,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: And shall perchance do good: but speak you on;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: You look as you had something more to say.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: If there be more, more woeful, hold it in;

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: For I am almost ready to dissolve,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Hearing of this.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: This would have seem’d a period

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: To such as love not sorrow; but another,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: To amplify too much, would make much more,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: And top extremity.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Whilst I was big in clamour came there in a man,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Who, having seen me in my worst estate,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Shunn’d my abhorr’d society; but then, finding

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Who ’twas that so endured, with his strong arms

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: He fastened on my neck, and bellow’d out

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: As he’d burst heaven; threw him on my father;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Told the most piteous tale of Lear and him

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: That ever ear received: which in recounting

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: His grief grew puissant and the strings of life

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Began to crack: twice then the trumpets sounded,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: And there I left him tranced.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: But who was this?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Kent, sir, the banish’d Kent; who in disguise

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Follow’d his enemy king, and did him service

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Improper for a slave.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Help, help, O, help!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: What kind of help?

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Speak, man.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: What means that bloody knife?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: ’Tis hot, it smokes;

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: It came even from the heart of—O, she’s dead!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Who dead? speak, man.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Your lady, sir, your lady: and her sister

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: By her is poisoned; she hath confess’d it.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I was contracted to them both: all three

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Now marry in an instant.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Here comes Kent.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Produce their bodies, be they alive or dead:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: This judgment of the heavens, that makes us tremble,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Touches us not with pity.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: O, is this he?

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: The time will not allow the compliment

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Which very manners urges.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I am come

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To bid my king and master aye good night:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Is he not here?

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Great thing of us forgot!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Speak, Edmund, where’s the king? and where’s Cordelia?

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: See’st thou this object, Kent?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Alack, why thus?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Yet Edmund was beloved:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: The one the other poison’d for my sake,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: And after slew herself.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Even so. Cover their faces.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I pant for life: some good I mean to do,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Despite of mine own nature. Quickly send,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Be brief in it, to the castle; for my writ

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Is on the life of Lear and on Cordelia:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Nay, send in time.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Run, run, O, run!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: To who, my lord? Who hath the office? send

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Thy token of reprieve.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Well thought on: take my sword,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Give it the captain.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Haste thee, for thy life.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: He hath commission from thy wife and me

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: To hang Cordelia in the prison, and

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: To lay the blame upon her own despair,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: That she fordid herself.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: The gods defend her! Bear him hence awhile. Edmund is borne off.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Howl, howl, howl, howl! O, you are men of stones:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Had I your tongues and eyes, I’ld use them so

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That heaven’s vault should crack. She’s gone for ever!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I know when one is dead, and when one lives;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: She’s dead as earth. Lend me a looking-glass;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: If that her breath will mist or stain the stone,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why, then she lives.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Is this the promised end?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Or image of that horror?

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Fall, and cease!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: This feather stirs; she lives! if it be so,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: It is a chance which does redeem all sorrows

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That ever I have felt.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: O my good master!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Prithee, away.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: ’Tis noble Kent, your friend.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: A plague upon you, murderers, traitors all!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I might have saved her; now she’s gone for ever!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Cordelia, Cordelia! stay a little. Ha!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What is’t thou say’st? Her voice was ever soft,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Gentle, and low, an excellent thing in woman.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I kill’d the slave that was a-hanging thee.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: ’Tis true, my lords, he did.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Did I not, fellow?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I have seen the day, with my good biting falchion

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I would have made them skip: I am old now,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And these same crosses spoil me. Who are you?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Mine eyes are not o’ the best: I’ll tell you straight.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: If fortune brag of two she loved and hated,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: One of them we behold.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: This is a dull sight. Are you not Kent?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The same,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Your servant Kent: Where is your servant Caius?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: He’s a good fellow, I can tell you that;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: He’ll strike, and quickly too: he’s dead and rotten.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: No, my good lord; I am the very man—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I’ll see that straight.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That, from your first of difference and decay,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Have follow’d your sad steps.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You are welcome hither.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Nor no man else: all’s cheerless, dark, and deadly.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Your eldest daughters have fordone them selves,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: And desperately are dead.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Ay, so I think.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: He knows not what he says: and vain it is

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: That we present us to him.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Very bootless.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: Edmund is dead, my lord.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: That’s but a trifle here.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: You lords and noble friends, know our intent.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: What comfort to this great decay may come

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Shall be applied: for us we will resign,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: During the life of this old majesty,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: To him our absolute power: To Edgar and Kent. you, to your rights:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: With boot, and such addition as your honours

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Have more than merited. All friends shall taste

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: The wages of their virtue, and all foes

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: The cup of their deservings. O, see, see!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And my poor fool is hang’d! No, no, no life!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why should a dog, a horse, a rat, have life,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And thou no breath at all? Thou’lt come no more,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Never, never, never, never, never!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Pray you, undo this button: thank you, sir.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Do you see this? Look on her, look, her lips,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Look there, look there!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: He faints! My lord, my lord!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Break, heart; I prithee, break!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Look up, my lord.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Vex not his ghost: O, let him pass! he hates him much

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That would upon the rack of this tough world

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Stretch him out longer.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: He is gone, indeed.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The wonder is, he hath endured so long:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: He but usurp’d his life.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Bear them from hence. Our present business

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Is general woe. To Kent and Edgar. Friends of my soul, you twain

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Rule in this realm, and the gored state sustain.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I have a journey, sir, shortly to go;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: My master calls me, I must not say no.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: The weight of this sad time we must obey;

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Speak what we feel, not what we ought to say.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: The oldest hath borne most: we that are young

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Shall never see so much, nor live so long.

stage_directions.text: Enter, in conquest, with drum and colours, Edmund, King Lear and Cordelia, prisoners; Captain, Soldiers, etc.
Exeunt King Lear and Cordelia, guarded.
Flourish. Enter Albany, Goneril, Regan, another Captain, and Soldiers.
Come hither, herald—Let the trumpet sound, And read out this.
Enter Edgar, at the third sound, armed, with a trumpet before him.
Enter a Gentleman, with a bloody knife.
The bodies of Goneril and Regan are brought in.
Reenter King Lear, with Cordelia dead in his arms; Edgar, Captain, and others following.

glosses.definition: Edmund's order to take Lear and Cordelia into custody
Lear's tender image of his coming imprisonment with Cordelia — love as enclosure, not loss

glosses.phrase: When thou dost ask me blessing, I’ll kneel down / And ask of thee forgiveness
glosses.definition: Lear's complete inversion of patriarchal hierarchy — father to daughter on his knees
gaudy courtiers — Lear and Cordelia will laugh at them from the cage

glosses.phrase: great ones / That ebb and flow by the moon
glosses.definition: the fickle powerful whose fortunes wax and wane
the secret of existence — Lear imagines he and Cordelia will see to its depth

glosses.phrase: packs and sects of great ones
glosses.definition: political factions
Lear's ferocity — they will resist enforced separation
whoever tries to separate us — Lear's promise to resist
Edmund gives secret orders to hang Cordelia
as circumstances require
Albany commends Edmund's battlefield conduct — unaware of his treacheries
stained like a toad's back — Albany's heraldic-grotesque insult for Goneril
Edgar, in full armor, answers the challenge and fights Edmund
the wanted-poster for Edgar — now overturned
Edgar narrates his and Gloucester's sufferings
Gloucester's heart 'burst smilingly' when Edgar revealed himself — grief and joy together

glosses.phrase: some good I mean to do
glosses.definition: Edmund's dying wish — revoke the order to kill Cordelia
proof to stop the execution
Albany's desperate command
killed herself — Edmund's report of Goneril's suicide
Lear entering with Cordelia's body — four repetitions of a single word; commonly called the most devastating line in Shakespeare
Lear's rebuke of everyone present — if they had any heart they would cry the vault of heaven apart
the sky should shatter from grief
Lear's immediate statement of the truth he will spend the rest of the scene refusing
Lear hopes for a sign of breath — puts a feather at Cordelia's mouth
Lear's term of endearment for Cordelia — 'fool' as beloved, NOT a reference to the Fool of the earlier acts

glosses.phrase: Why should a dog, a horse, a rat, have life, / And thou no breath at all?
glosses.definition: the most famous theological protest in the play — the bare injustice of Cordelia's death against animal survival
Lear's incantation against hope
Lear's fivefold 'never' — the inverse of the fourfold 'Howl'; two numerical extremes bracketing his collapse
Lear's dying request — his collar is choking him; the smallness of the gesture is what makes it unbearable
Lear dies believing he sees Cordelia alive — the play's final ambiguity about death and perception
the instrument of torture — the world stretching him to breaking

glosses.phrase: The weight of this sad time we must obey; / Speak what we feel, not what we ought to say
glosses.definition: the play's closing couplet — in some editions spoken by Edgar (who becomes king), in others by Albany (who steps aside); the speaker-attribution is a textual crux that shapes interpretation
the old suffered most

glosses.phrase: we that are young / Shall never see so much, nor live so long
glosses.definition: the final line — the survivors will never endure what has passed, nor match the elders in years
strict custody
Lear reversing the filial gesture
gaudy court hangers-on
the 'sacrifices' upon which the gods cast incense — Lear imagines their imprisonment as sacred
Lear's promise — whoever separates us must fire them out like foxes
a great matter we forgot
judgment — Albany's on Edmund
equal; co-regent
Albany on Goneril — heraldic-grotesque insult
within the dueling enclosure
the charges Edgar reads against Edmund

glosses.phrase: what safe and nicely I might well delay
glosses.definition: what I might prudently refuse
let us be reconciled
to flee the wanted-poster
Goneril — poison confession
Lear's cry over Cordelia
Lear's name for himself and Cordelia — eavesdroppers on divine purposes
Lear's ferocity — they will resist by starvation before separation
perjured — Edgar's charges against Edmund
formal declaration
forgive and be forgiven — Edgar to the dying Edmund
powerful
destroyed; killed
Edgar: this is only a picture of the promised end, not the end itself

annotations.title: 'We two alone will sing like birds i' the cage' — Lear's fantasy of enclosed love
annotations.body: Captured by Edmund's forces with Cordelia, Lear imagines prison as idyll: 'We two alone will sing like birds i' the cage.' This is the scene's most tender moment before its destruction. Lear imagines an endless father-daughter companionship — kneeling for blessings, praying, telling stories, laughing at court gossip 'as if we were God's spies.' The speech compresses everything he lost at 1.1 and found at 4.7. It is also willfully unreal; he is imagining a prison that will never exist because he cannot protect Cordelia from Edmund's order. Shakespeare lets the speech run to thirty lines of dreamy beauty so the audience can inhabit it before he breaks it. When Edmund immediately orders Cordelia's execution fifteen lines later, the speech is retroactively a eulogy.
sources: Stanley Cavell, 'The Avoidance of Love' in Disowning Knowledge (1987)
Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)

annotations.title: 'Howl, howl, howl, howl!' — four repetitions of one word
annotations.body: Lear enters with Cordelia's body and says 'Howl, howl, howl, howl! O, you are men of stones.' Four repetitions of a single word — commonly identified as the most devastating line in Shakespeare. The word 'howl' is pre-linguistic grief: not an idea but an utterance. Its fourfold repetition refuses to resolve into meaning; it is the sound of a human being at the end of his capacity for words. Later in the speech Lear will say 'Never, never, never, never, never' — five repetitions of 'never' mirror the four 'Howl's, the pair bracketing his collapse. 'Why should a dog, a horse, a rat, have life, / And thou no breath at all?' — this is the play's most bitter theodicy, delivered as arithmetic: three living animals, one dead Cordelia. And the sequence ends not with a cry but with 'Pray you, undo this button' — a domestic, small request that concentrates grief into a smaller event than the 'Howl' could carry. Shakespeare's most famous closing sequence is built on this asymmetry: the howl that cannot be spoken and the button that can.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: Nahum Tate's happy ending — 150 years of Lear without death
annotations.body: In 1681 Nahum Tate published 'The History of King Lear,' an adaptation in which Cordelia does not die, she marries Edgar, Lear and Gloucester both survive, and the play ends happily. Tate's Lear became the ONLY version performed on the English stage for 150 years. Samuel Johnson, in his 1765 edition of Shakespeare, defended Tate's version — he wrote that he was so shocked by Cordelia's death that he could not re-read the final scenes until he had to edit them. Charles Lamb in his 1811 essay 'On the Tragedies of Shakespeare' attacked Tate's version as destroying the play's moral argument. It was Edmund Kean's 1823 production that finally restored Shakespeare's tragic ending to the London stage, and only in 1838 (Macready's production) was Tate's version fully retired. The fact that the English theatrical tradition could not bear Shakespeare's ending for 150 years is itself part of the play's meaning. Cordelia's death is designed to be unbearable, and the tradition confirmed it by refusing to bear it. Virgil's verdict: if you feel the wrongness of Cordelia's death, you are having the correct response, and the whole critical tradition is with you.
sources: Nahum Tate, The History of King Lear (1681)
Charles Lamb, 'On the Tragedies of Shakespeare' (1811)
Michael Dobson, The Making of the National Poet (1992)

annotations.title: The final couplet — Edgar or Albany?
annotations.body: 'The weight of this sad time we must obey; / Speak what we feel, not what we ought to say. / The oldest hath borne most: we that are young / Shall never see so much, nor live so long.' In the 1623 Folio (F1), these final lines are spoken by EDGAR; in the 1608 Quarto (Q1), they are spoken by ALBANY. This is one of the most consequential speaker-attribution differences in the Shakespeare canon — not because of any particular textual detail, but because who speaks the play's closing lines determines what the play closes as. If Edgar speaks them, the young survivor is taking authority — a new king stepping forward. If Albany speaks them, the middle-aged survivor is stepping aside, giving the final word to youth even while disclaiming office. Most modern editors give the lines to Edgar (following F1), but the assignment is not settled. The brief's flag of this crux is well-placed: readers should know the play ends with textual uncertainty about its own successor.
sources: Gary Taylor and Michael Warren (eds.), The Division of the Kingdoms (1983)
Stanley Wells and Gary Taylor, William Shakespeare: A Textual Companion (1987)

annotations.title: 'Never, never, never, never, never' — pentameter collapse
annotations.body: Lear's fivefold 'never' is a perfect iambic pentameter line ('ne-ver, ne-ver, ne-ver, ne-ver, ne-ver' = five trochees, or five stressed monosyllables depending on reading), made of nothing but one word. Shakespeare takes the formal unit of his verse — the iambic pentameter line — and fills it with a single syllable of negation. This is meter at its most self-abolishing: the line stays a line, but its content has collapsed into the smallest unit. Critics sometimes call this Shakespeare's most concentrated moment of form expressing content. The verse is doing the work Lear's language can no longer do — holding the shape of speech around a void of meaning. This is the companion line to the four 'Howl's: negation and grief as counterweights to the rest of the play's verbal richness.
sources: George T. Wright, Shakespeare's Metrical Art (1988)

annotations.title: The duel and Edgar's unmasking — the Gloucester plot's catharsis
annotations.body: Edgar finally defeats Edmund in armored combat and unmasks. 'Let's exchange charity.' The moment formally resolves the Gloucester subplot: the legitimate son has survived, the bastard is dying, the father (who has died offstage with his 'heart burst smilingly') has been avenged. But the resolution is immediately dwarfed by what follows: Cordelia's death and Lear's collapse. Shakespeare is doing something specific in the scene's sequencing — the Gloucester plot gets a classical catharsis while the Lear plot refuses one. The subplot rewards its virtuous survivor; the main plot does not. That asymmetry is the play's final structural argument: there is no general rule that virtue is rewarded; the Gloucester plot was the luck of a single survivor, and the main plot shows what happens when luck runs out.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: Kent's refusal to live — the loyal servant asks leave
annotations.body: As the survivors gather, Kent — who has served Lear in disguise for nearly the entire play — says 'I have a journey, sir, shortly to go; / My master calls me, I must not say no.' Kent is announcing his own impending death, following Lear. Albany's attempt to share the rule ('Friends of my soul, you twain / Rule in this realm, and the gor'd state sustain') is refused: Kent will not survive, Edgar will stand alone (or with Albany). The play will not permit its oldest loyal witness to live past his master. Kent's loyalty is absolute, and the play honors it by refusing him a later career. There is no restoration-narrative for those who loved Lear completely.

trials.prompt: Why is 'Howl, howl, howl, howl!' considered the most devastating line in Shakespeare?
options: It is the longest line in the play
Four repetitions of a single pre-linguistic word of grief — a human being at the end of his capacity for meaning; refusing to resolve into sense

trials.prompt: What was Nahum Tate's 1681 adaptation of King Lear, and why does it matter?
options: A happy-ending rewrite (Cordelia lives, marries Edgar) that became the ONLY Lear performed in English theaters for 150 years, until 1838 — showing the tradition could not bear Shakespeare's ending, which is itself part of the play's meaning

trials.prompt: The final couplet of King Lear ('The weight of this sad time...') is spoken by whom?
options: Always by Albany in all editions
Edgar in the 1623 Folio, Albany in the 1608 Quarto — a textual crux whose resolution changes what the play closes as (a new king stepping forward, or a survivor stepping aside)

trials.prompt: What is formally remarkable about 'Never, never, never, never, never'?
options: A perfect iambic-pentameter-length line built from a single word of negation — Shakespeare's form holding the shape of speech around a void of meaning
It rhymes with the next line

trials.prompt: Lear's 'Why should a dog, a horse, a rat, have life, / And thou no breath at all?' is —
options: The play's bitterest theodicy — arithmetic of injustice: three living animals vs. one dead Cordelia; the gods' accounting fails

trials.prompt: What is the significance of Lear's final request 'Pray you, undo this button'?
options: The smallness of the gesture — domestic, bodily, almost embarrassed — concentrates grief into an event smaller than 'Howl' can carry; Shakespeare's most compressed ending
He is asking for his crown back
He is asking to be hanged

trials.prompt: How do the Gloucester and Lear plots resolve differently in 5.3?
options: Gloucester plot receives classical catharsis (Edgar survives, Edmund dies, Gloucester's heart 'burst smilingly'); Lear plot refuses catharsis (Cordelia dies, Lear collapses) — the asymmetry argues that virtue is not generally rewarded

trials.prompt: What does Kent's 'I have a journey, sir, shortly to go' indicate?
options: He is announcing his own impending death — he will follow his master Lear and not remain to rule; the loyal witness refuses a later career
He is returning to his own estates
